description: A comprehensive training program for building autonomous drone systems designed to detect, track, classify, and neutralize unauthorized drones in protected airspace. The program covers all four engineering tracks—AI, MLOps, Data Engineering, and Edge AI—applied specifically to counter‑UAS (C‑UAS) operations, including radar/RF sensor fusion, pursuit‑evasion algorithms, and regulatory compliance.
learning_objectives: Understand the threat landscape: rogue drone categories, payload risks, and airspace vulnerabilities
Build AI models for drone detection, classification, and intent inference using multi‑sensor fusion
Design real‑time geospatial data pipelines for multi‑sensor C‑UAS systems
Deploy low‑latency detection and tracking models on edge hardware for rapid response
Implement pursuit‑evasion algorithms and safe intercept planning
Operate within legal and regulatory frameworks for C‑UAS deployments
Continuously retrain models as adversarial drone designs evolve

lessons.title: Threat Landscape and Drone Categories
lessons.content: Classify rogue drones by size (nano, micro, small, large), payload risk (surveillance, EMP, explosive), and operator intent (accidental incursion, deliberate attack, smuggling). Study historical incidents at airports, stadiums, and government facilities. Understand attacker TTPs (tactics, techniques, procedures): low‑altitude nap‑of‑earth flight, GPS spoofing, RF silence modes. Define protected airspace categories: geofenced zones, temporary flight restrictions (TFRs), and critical infrastructure corridors.

lessons.title: Sensor Modalities for Detection
lessons.content: Survey C‑UAS sensor types: radar (primary radar, secondary radar, Doppler), RF/spectrum analyzers (passive 2.4/5.8 GHz, DJI OcuSync, Wi‑Fi), acoustic arrays, electro‑optical (EO) cameras, infrared (IR) cameras, and LiDAR. Understand detection range, false‑positive rates, and weather sensitivity of each. Study multi‑sensor fusion strategies to improve detection probability while reducing false alarms. Explore commercially available integrated C‑UAS systems (DroneShield, Dedrone, Fortem SkyDome) as references.

modules.title: Module 2: AI Engineer – Detection, Classification, and Tracking

lessons.title: Radar‑Based Drone Detection with Micro‑Doppler Signatures
lessons.content: Use micro‑Doppler radar signatures to distinguish drones from birds and clutter. Extract time‑frequency spectrograms from radar I/Q data. Train a CNN classifier on spectrogram images to classify: quadcopter, fixed‑wing, helicopter, bird, or background. Evaluate with ROC curves; target >95% TPR at <1% FPR. Handle variable drone orientations and speeds.
lessons.code_example:
import numpy as np
from scipy.signal import spectrogram
# I/Q data from radar
f, t, Sxx = spectrogram(iq_data, fs=1e6, window='hann', nperseg=256)
# Normalize and feed to CNN classifier
img = np.log10(Sxx + 1e-10)
pred = cnn_model.predict(img[np.newaxis, :, :, np.newaxis])

lessons.title: RF Fingerprinting for Drone Identification
lessons.content: Capture RF emissions from drone control links (2.4 GHz, 5.8 GHz, 900 MHz). Use an SDR (Software Defined Radio, e.g., HackRF One) to record raw IQ samples. Extract features: burst timing, modulation scheme, frequency hopping pattern. Train a classifier to identify drone make/model (DJI Phantom, Autel, FPV racing drone). Use siamese networks for few‑shot identification of new drone types.
lessons.code_example:
from gnuradio import gr, blocks, analog
# Capture IQ samples at target frequency
# Extract burst-level features
import torch
from siamese_net import DroneRFNet
model = DroneRFNet()
embedding = model(iq_tensor)  # compare to known fingerprints

lessons.title: Visual Tracking with Re‑Identification
lessons.content: Build a UAV tracking pipeline: detect drones with a fine‑tuned YOLO model on high‑altitude EO video. Use ByteTrack or BotSort for multi‑object tracking across frames. Implement Re‑ID to maintain identity when a drone leaves and re‑enters the camera frame. Fuse with radar azimuth/elevation to produce 3D tracks. Predict future positions using a Kalman filter for intercept planning.
lessons.code_example:
from ultralytics import YOLO
from bytetrack import BYTETracker
model = YOLO('drone_detector_yolov8n.pt')
tracker = BYTETracker(track_thresh=0.5, match_thresh=0.8)
results = model(frame)
tracks = tracker.update(results.boxes.data, frame.shape)

modules.title: Module 3: Data Engineer – Multi‑Sensor C‑UAS Data Pipeline

lessons.title: Geospatial Track Storage with PostGIS
lessons.content: Store real‑time drone tracks as PostGIS geometry objects. Each track record includes: timestamp, sensor source, geom (Point 3D with altitude), velocity vector, classification label, and confidence score. Use ST_MakeLine to reconstruct flight paths. Enable sub‑second inserts using prepared statements and connection pooling. Query: 'all tracks within 500 m of runway threshold in last 60 seconds'.

lessons.title: Real‑Time Sensor Fusion with Apache Kafka
lessons.content: Stream radar, RF, EO, and acoustic detections into separate Kafka topics. Use a Flink consumer to join streams within a 500 ms sliding window, matching detections by spatial proximity. Output fused tracks to a unified 'verified_tracks' topic. This architecture decouples sensor systems and allows horizontal scaling as sensor count grows.

lessons.title: Incident Reporting and Evidence Storage (STAC)
lessons.content: When a rogue drone incident is confirmed, auto‑generate an incident record: video clip from EO camera, radar spectrograms, RF captures, and track GeoJSON. Package as a STAC item with properties: incident_id, threat_level, drone_class, first_detection, resolution (scared off, jammed, captured). Store in object storage for legal evidence and model improvement.

modules.title: Module 4: Edge AI Engineer – Low‑Latency Detection on Interceptor Drone

lessons.title: Hardware for Interceptor Drone
lessons.content: Configure an interceptor drone: high‑speed frame (7‑inch, 6S LiPo for 120+ kph), forward‑facing EO+IR camera, onboard Jetson Orin NX for inference, and directional RF jammer (where legally permitted). Connect to Pixhawk flight controller via MAVLink. Use a companion computer link (USB serial or Ethernet) for high‑bandwidth sensor data. Key requirement: total detection‑to‑intercept latency < 2 seconds.

lessons.title: TensorRT‑Optimized Drone Detector
lessons.content: Export the YOLO drone detector to TensorRT FP16. Achieve 60+ fps at 640×640 on Jetson Orin NX. Profile with Nsight Systems to identify bottlenecks. Implement a frame‑skipping strategy: run full detection every other frame, use optical flow to update bounding boxes on skipped frames, maintaining perceived 60 fps with 30 fps inference cost.
lessons.code_example:
# Export to TensorRT
from ultralytics import YOLO
model = YOLO('drone_detector.pt')
model.export(format='engine', device=0, half=True, imgsz=640)
# Runtime
import tensorrt as trt
# Load engine and run inference at 60+ fps

lessons.title: Pursuit‑Evasion Navigation with ROS 2
lessons.content: Implement a pursuit node: subscribe to '/target_track' (3D position + velocity). Compute intercept point using predictive guidance (proportional navigation law). Publish velocity setpoints to MAVROS. Add safety constraints: maximum intercept speed (80% of top speed), minimum altitude (30 m AGL), geo‑fence exit triggers return‑to‑home. Simulate in AirSim before hardware testing.

modules.title: Module 5: ML Platform Engineer – Adversarial Adaptation

lessons.title: Active Learning on Novel Drone Types
lessons.content: When a new drone model appears in the field, it may evade existing classifiers. Set up a pipeline: detections with low confidence or misclassifications are flagged. RF captures and video clips are sent to operators for labeling. New samples are added to training data; the model is retrained nightly using LoRA fine‑tuning on the base classifier. The cycle ensures adaptation within 24‑48 hours of encountering a new drone.
lessons.code_example:
uncertain = [d for d in detections if d['confidence'] < 0.6]
labelbox_client.create_task('novel_drone_review', uncertain)
# After labeling, trigger retraining pipeline

lessons.title: A/B Testing Countermeasure Strategies
lessons.content: Use MLflow to track effectiveness of countermeasures: RF jamming success rate, visual deterrence (directed lights/sound) success rate, and interceptor capture rate. Run A/B tests across different protected zones to determine which countermeasure is most effective for each threat class. Feed results back to the decision model that selects countermeasures.
lessons.code_example:
import mlflow
with mlflow.start_run(run_name='countermeasure_ab_test'):
    mlflow.log_param('strategy_a', 'rf_jamming')
    mlflow.log_param('strategy_b', 'visual_deterrence')
    mlflow.log_metric('success_rate_a', 0.87)
    mlflow.log_metric('success_rate_b', 0.61)

modules.title: Module 6: Safety, Legal, and Regulatory Compliance

lessons.title: Legal Framework for C‑UAS Operations
lessons.content: Understand legal constraints: in most jurisdictions, kinetic countermeasures (nets, bullets) require military/law enforcement authority. RF jamming is classified as interference with communications (FCC Part 97 in US; Article 326 in Germany) and requires specific authorization. Passive detection and visual deterrence are generally lawful. Study FAA reauthorization acts (2018, 2024) enabling C‑UAS at airports, stadiums, and federal buildings. EASA U‑space and UK CAA frameworks for Europe and UK.

lessons.title: Operational Protocols and Escalation Procedures
lessons.content: Define a tiered response protocol: Level 1 (detect, alert operator), Level 2 (attempt contact/RF deterrence), Level 3 (deploy interceptor), Level 4 (kinetic — law enforcement only). Log every action with timestamp, operator ID, and outcome for regulatory reporting. Conduct monthly tabletop exercises simulating rogue drone incursions to test response times and decision accuracy.

capstone_project.name: Autonomous C‑UAS Sentinel System
capstone_project.description: Build an integrated counter‑drone system that uses radar micro‑Doppler signatures and RF fingerprinting to detect and classify rogue drones within a 1 km radius, tracks them with a visual interceptor drone, and logs all incidents as STAC items for evidence and model improvement. The system must detect a DJI Phantom‑class drone in <3 seconds from first radar return and maintain track through evasive maneuvers.
deliverables: Trained radar micro‑Doppler CNN classifier (>95% TPR at <1% FPR)
RF fingerprinting model with siamese network architecture
ROS 2 pursuit‑evasion node with proportional navigation
Compliance checklist for C‑UAS deployment in target jurisdiction
